entry.title: Re: SAP CPQ Set Product Price dynamically from attribute...
entry.content:
Hi @vromeo30 ,

 

Based on the screenshots, the Test value is correctly entered on the configurable product. However, the pricing formula <*VALUE(Test)*> is evaluated in the context of the referenced simple product, which does not automatically receive the configurable product's value, as it does not share the same attribute context.

Value inheritance in SAP CPQ is supported within a true parent/child bundle structure between configurable items. In your case, you are referencing a simple product inside a configurable product, and that setup does not provide automatic attribute value inheritance into the simple product's pricing context.

 

The simple product evaluates its own attribute state. Since its Test attribute is not populated at pricing time, the formula returns 0. While simple products can have attributes (including preselected values), they do not automatically inherit attribute values from the configurable product.

In this scenario, the most reliable solution is to use scripting (e.g. On Product Complete, On Rule Execution End, or a Change Trigger on the Test attribute) to explicitly assign the simple product's price based on the configurable product's Test value. This ensures deterministic pricing behaviour rather than relying on implicit propagation.

Hope this clarifies the behaviour.

entry.title: Re: Display configuration values by user login language i...
entry.content:
Hi @burcu_kumlar ,

In addition to the language-dependent configuration settings already mentioned, and assuming that all attribute value translations are properly maintained in the system (a prerequisite for the translation engine to function correctly), there are two important options in Document Generation -> General Settings that should be verified.

 

First, ensure that "Translate quotes using the user's default dictionary" is enabled. This setting allows the system to generate documents based on the logged-in user's dictionary, ensuring that configuration values are rendered in the appropriate language where translations are available.

Second, consider enabling "Users can choose customer's language when creating documents". This provides flexibility during document generation by allowing the user to explicitly select the output language. This is particularly useful when the customer's primary language differs from the user's login language.

 

When both settings are correctly configured, SAP CPQ will resolve configuration values according to the selected language context during document generation, ensuring controlled and predictable multilingual document output in Quote 2.0

entry.title: The Future of SAP ABAP Based Systems with AI: Why MCP Servers Matter
entry.content:
What if your AI assistant could actually talk to your SAP system?

Not through screenshots. Not through copy-paste. But real, direct communication where you ask "Help me find and analyze all enhancements related to Sales Process" or "Give me an RCA document for the ABAP dump I saw in production" and get an instant answer pulled straight from your SAP environment.

This isn't science fiction. It's happening now, thanks to Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers.

The Problem We've All Been Ignoring

Let's be honest: for all the hype about AI transforming enterprise software, most of us are still using ChatGPT and Claude like glorified search engines. We describe our SAP problems, get generic advice, then manually dig through transactions to find what we need.

The disconnect is painful. AI assistants are incredibly smart, but they're blind to our actual code. They can't see your complex pricing procedures, can't check your vendor master data, can't analyze your cash flow in real-time.

Until now.

Enter MCP: The Missing Link

To understand why MCP matters, let's take a step back and look at how we've traditionally communicated with SAP systems.

The Evolution of SAP Communication

In the beginning, there was DIAG (Dynamic Information and Action Gateway) the proprietary protocol that allowed SAP GUI to communicate with SAP servers. It worked, but it was closed, SAP specific, and limited to the GUI client. If you wanted to interact with SAP, you needed SAP GUI.

Then came the shift toward modern development tools. SAP introduced the ABAP Development Tools (ADT) framework - the foundation that powers ABAP in Eclipse. ADT brought a crucial innovation: a standardized way for external tools to communicate with SAP systems over HTTP/HTTPS, exposing SAP functionality through REST-like APIs.

ADT framework opened doors:

 

  • Eclipse could retrieve code, metadata, and system information
  • External tools could trigger ABAP unit tests, syntax checks, and activations
  • Developers got modern IDE features while working with SAP systems

 

And this is exactly what Vibing Steampunk by Alice Vinogradova leverages.

The MCP server uses the same ADT framework that Eclipse uses to communicate with SAP. It's not reinventing the wheel. it's standing on SAP's own modern integration foundation.

But Here's Where MCP Takes It Further

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that lets AI assistants connect directly to external systems. Think of it as a universal translator between AI and enterprise applications.

While ADT framework solved the "how to communicate with SAP" problem, MCP solves the "how to make AI understand and interact with SAP" problem.

Instead of you being the middleman between AI and SAP, the AI can:

 

  • Query SAP system metadata through ADT endpoints
  • Interpret the results in business context
  • Give you actionable insights in natural language

 

The combination is powerful: ADT provides the technical connectivity, MCP provides the intelligent interface.

Real Example: Vibing Steampunk MCP Server

I recently discovered (and have been experimenting with) a project called Vibing Steampunk on GitHub. It's an MCP server specifically built for communicating with ABAP-based systems.

But here's where it gets interesting for developers: this isn't just about querying data. It's about how AI can help you build and understand SAP systems.

Scenario: Understanding a BAPI

Before MCP:

 

  • You open SE37 → Search for BAPIs → Read documentation → Check parameters → Test in SE37
  • Time: 20-30 minutes of navigation and documentation reading

 

With MCP:

 

  • You (to AI): "Show me the BAPI parameters for creating outgoing payments and explain the mandatory fields"
  • AI queries SAP directly → Retrieves BAPI metadata → Explains in context
  • Time: 30 seconds

 

Scenario: Code Analysis

Before MCP:

 

  • You inherit a custom payment program
  • You trace through ABAP code → Check database tables → Understand data flow → Document logic
  • Time: Hours or days depending on complexity

 

With MCP:

 

  • You: "Analyze this payment posting program and explain the data flow from BAPI_XXXXXX to the database tables"
  • AI queries SAP for BAPI structure → Examines table relationships → Maps the data flow → Explains in plain language
  • Time: Minutes

 

Why This Matters for ABAP Developers

1. Faster Onboarding

MCP enabled AI guides new developers through your SAP landscape showing payment BAPI locations, custom table structures, and organizational extensions dramatically reducing the learning curve.

2. Living Documentation

Forget stale documents. AI queries your actual SAP system for real-time information on BAPI signatures, custom table relationships, function module usage, and data element definitions.

3. Intelligent Code Assistance

Beyond GitHub Copilot, AI understands your environment:

 

  • "Show payment blocks in REGUH"
  • "Explain BSEG-payment document relationships"
  • "Find programs calling BAPI_XXXXXX"

 

It queries your system for accurate, environment-specific answers.

4. Faster Debugging

Skip manual tracing through BAPI dumps, SLG1 logs, and database states. Ask "Why did payment 12345 fail?" and AI retrieves error logs, checks document structure, and explains the issue.

5. Knowledge Transfer

Preserve senior developer expertise. MCP enabled AI explains custom code rationale, shows configuration context, and guides juniors through complex payment scenarios using real system data.

Important: The AI never executes code directly in SAP it requests information through the MCP server, which enforces all authorization checks and logs every action.

What This Means for the Industry

We're at an inflection point. SAP has announced Joule, their AI copilot. Microsoft has Copilot integrated across Dynamics and Power Platform. Oracle has embedded AI across their cloud suite.

But here's what makes MCP different: it's open and extensible.

You're not locked into one vendor's AI. You can:

 

  • Use the AI assistant you prefer (Claude, ChatGPT, local models)
  • Customize the integration for your specific business processes
  • Build proprietary MCP servers for competitive advantage
  • Integrate multiple systems (SAP + Salesforce + your data warehouse)

 

The Bigger Picture for Development Teams

This isn't just about making development faster. It's about fundamentally changing how we build and maintain SAP systems.

Imagine:

Onboarding: New ABAP developers asking "How does our payment posting process work?" and getting a complete explanation with actual code references and data flows from your system

Code Reviews: AI assistant examining your BAPI wrapper code and saying "This won't work for payment method 'C' because field REGUH-RZALW is mandatory but not populated"

Impact Analysis: Before modifying a payment table structure, asking "What custom programs will be affected by changing table ZPAYMENT_LOG?" and getting a complete dependency map

Documentation: AI automatically generating technical specs by analyzing your actual implementation, not from templates

Troubleshooting: During production issues, asking "Show me all failed payment postings in the last hour with their error messages" and getting analyzed results with suggested fixes

We're moving from "AI that knows ABAP syntax" to "AI that understands YOUR SAP system."

That's not a minor upgrade. That's a paradigm shift in how we develop, maintain, and transfer knowledge in SAP environments.

Your Turn

Have you experimented with connecting AI assistants to your SAP development environment? What repetitive tasks would you want AI to help with? What concerns do you have about AI accessing system metadata?

I'm genuinely curious to hear from the ABAP community. The technology is ready. The question is: what development challenges will we solve with it?

Interested in the technical implementation? Check out the Vibing Steampunk MCP server on GitHub and let me know what you think. Let's build the future of SAP development together.

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
Great blog post @SreekanthSurampally - think it's quite important to have articles on the Object Store architecture, given its novelty in the overall Datasphere/BDC.

Think that there is still quite a lot confusion related to the Object Store vs. more 'older' HANA Data Lake (relational one), and which one should be used, for what exactly, how much they cost compared to each other and compared to the traditional HANA Cloud 'disk' storage etc.

Yet, what I still cannot fully comprehend - Object Store (HANA Data Lake on Files) costs roughly ~0.075 CU per GB-Month, while traditional HANA Cloud disk costs roughly ~0.18 CU per GB-Month (OK, the former requires also some compute and requests in addition, while the latter requires memory/compute). It's (very roughly, if we also disregard compression etc.) x2.4 cost rationing, and not say x10 rationing (that one might expect), for a non-negligible performance and modelling overhead.

To put things into perspective, in the same manner, the 'older' (relational engine) HANA Data Lake storage costs roughly ~0.56 CU per GB-Month (OK, compute included), which then makes it even more expensive than the HANA Cloud disk storage - cannot see then why would it be even ever used if more expensive and slower and more complex(?!)

Or maybe I'm missing some important points in reading the whole thing? 🙂

entry.title: Re: C_IEE2E Practical Exam Under Review → Attempt Closed...
entry.content:
Hi Taofiksanni, unfortunately I cannot answer your question directly but will share my experience with some technical issues I faced in my exam last Friday the 13th of Feb 26 on the Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting exam. 

First of all, I didn't have access to CBC, neither via a specific link and via "manage your solution" app, once clicked I got an "unauthorized" error in the new window, probably because of misconfiguration in IAS groups for CBC or login info. 

Secondly, the exam asks you to create the journal entries and documents with a reference like P###, swapping '###' for the number you're assigned, but this explanation if VERY poorly given, the only number I got was my login as 'F123456' and SAP doesn't give a clue if you need to swap 123 or 456 into this reference. Also, some questions ask you to create some objects (BPs, Cost Centers) with this naming convention, but some objects already exist, leaving you with no clue if you can use them or not.

The overall result was that I lost 30 minutes in the beginning of the exam and did my whole exam without being sure if I was submitting my answers correctly, I was quite confident with my answers but scored only 4% and obviously failed. I strongly believe the review system couldn't validate my answers. 

I opened a ticket to the support and will update everyone here. I hope to get some answers and get my attempt credit back and share my experience with other people who is about to take the same FICO exams in the future.

entry.title: India : Practical use inquiry on the "Invoice Reduction" function
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

Do you use the “Invoice Reduction” function in Create Supplier Invoice / MIRO in India?

I have also consulted Joule for Consultants; however, I am not in a position to confirm whether the responses fully reflect actual business practice. I would greatly appreciate your insights based on real project or customer experience in India, or a Joule-based answer that has been validated in practice.

Our intention is to understand the actual practice in India.

The background of this question relates to the “Invoice Reduction” function available during supplier invoice creation in SAP. We would like to understand whether this function is commonly used in India and whether it is typically applicable for customers there.

For reference, in Japan this function is generally not used. When there is a price difference at the time of invoicing, it is typically handled by either:

  • correcting the PO price, or using subsequent debit/credit functionality,

rather than directly reducing the invoice amount via the invoice reduction function.

Could you kindly share the common practice in India based on your experience?

Thank you very much in advance for your guidance.

entry.title: Re: Unable to add email domain
entry.content: As per KBA 3025172 - How to add/remove email domains - SAP for Me, only Super Administrators and Cloud Administrators can use the Maintain Allowed Company Domains application to register and maintain a list of official Internet domains for their company. Since you are neither a super admin nor a cloud admin, this application  Maintain Allowed Company Domains is not accessible for you. For data security reason, our SAP always recommend customer to manage the domain by themselves. Thus please contact below super admin or cloud admin to help add the domain.

entry.title: El reto real del CFO actual
entry.content:
La mayoría de las organizaciones aún operan con procesos financieros que:

  • Dependen de reconciliaciones manuales
  • Tienen información fragmentada entre FI, CO y módulos satélite
  • Cierran tarde y con alta presión operativa
  • Reaccionan, en lugar de anticipar

El problema no es el equipo. Es la arquitectura financiera heredada.

SAP S/4HANA: Finanzas en tiempo real, no al cierre del mes

SAP S/4HANA redefine el rol de Finanzas a partir de un concepto clave: el Universal Journal.

Esto permite:

  • Un solo registro financiero (ACDOCA)
  • Eliminación de reconciliaciones entre FI y CO
  • Información consistente desde el origen
  • Reportes en tiempo real, sin esperar al cierre

Para el CFO, esto se traduce en algo muy concreto: confianza en los números, en cualquier momento.

Cierre financiero más rápido, con menos esfuerzo

Uno de los mayores dolores del CFO es el cierre mensual.

Con SAP S/4HANA:

  • Se automatizan tareas repetitivas
  • Se reducen ajustes de último minuto
  • El cierre deja de ser un evento traumático

El beneficio no es solo cerrar más rápido, sino liberar tiempo del equipo financiero para análisis y estrategia.

De control operativo a visión estratégica

SAP S/4HANA permite a Finanzas:

  • Analizar rentabilidad en tiempo real
  • Monitorear flujo de efectivo de forma predictiva
  • Detectar desviaciones antes de que se conviertan en problemas
  • Integrar analítica directamente en los procesos (Embedded Analytics)

Esto cambia la conversación del CFO con la Dirección General: de explicar resultados a guiar decisiones.

Automatización e inteligencia: menos operación, más valor

S/4HANA, combinado con soluciones como SAP BTP, IA y herramientas de automatización, habilita:

  • Conciliaciones automáticas
  • Cash Application inteligente
  • Detección de anomalías
  • Forecast financiero más preciso

No se trata de reemplazar personas, sino de elevar el rol del talento financiero.

Cloud, control y escalabilidad

Para muchos CFOs, la pregunta no es si migrar, sino cómo hacerlo sin perder control.

SAP S/4HANA ofrece:

  • Modelos Cloud y On-Premise
  • Seguridad y cumplimiento normativo
  • Escalabilidad para crecer sin rediseñar Finanzas cada pocos años

La clave es elegir el camino correcto alineado al modelo de negocio.

 

#CFO #SAP #S4HANA #FinanzasEstratégicas #TransformacionFinanciera #ERP #AI

entry.title: One Delivery Order with multiple PO numbers generates two invoices
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
We’re encountering a scenario where one Delivery Order (DO) contains multiple PO numbers, but during billing, SAP generates two separate invoices with different billing document numbers, and both get printed.
Business expectation is to have a single consolidated invoice (single billing document / single print copy) for the same DO.
Ideally:

1 Delivery Order → 1 Invoice

Could you please advise:
1. What standard billing split criteria could cause this behavior when multiple PO numbers exist?
2. Which configurations should we review (e.g. VTFL, billing split analysis, header/item split criteria)?
3. Is this achievable via standard configuration, or would enhancement/user exit be required?

This is not SmartForms related. It appears to be standard SD billing logic.
Appreciate any guidance or similar experience
Thank you!

entry.title: Supply Protection Extension Rule in SAP S/4 HANA
entry.content:
In this blog we will see how supply protection extension rules can be configured to modify the consumption and restriction behavior of protection groups and their corresponding time buckets.

Pre-Requisites:

  • Product version is S/4 HANA Private Cloud Edition 2023 FPS03 / or above and Fashion business function is activated
  • Self-Restriction type is set as ‘Restriction Outside Planned Protection’

Solution Context:

Supply Protection Extension Rule aims in addressing below business requirements:

  1. Consider protected quantities of backward and forward time buckets while calculating remaining protection for the matching time buckets
  2. Consume protection from backward and forward time buckets
  3. Ignore restrictions applied due to horizontal protection up to remaining protection and consider them only for the required quantity exceeding remaining protection.

Below Example illustrates a scenario where Supply Protection Extension Rules are crucial in defining the consumption from multiple time buckets.

 

 

Feb

Mar

Apr

May

 

Supply

 

 

On Hand Stock

250

 

 

 

 

Inbound Delivery

 

225

 

 

 

Purchase Order

 

 

225

 

 

Purchase Requisition

 

 

 

225

 

 

 

Supply Protection

 

Priority

Protection Group

 

 

 

 

1

ECOM

50

50

50

50

1

RETAIL

75

75

75

75

2

Key Wholesale

100

100

100

100

 

General Availability

25

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Its important to set the right “Self-Restriction” Type in above case to ensure a specific channel doesn’t overconsume the supply. For e.g. if we set “Self-Restriction” as “No Self-Restriction” then ECOM and RETAIL channels can consume all of key wholesale protection which is not always intended.

We will review this example with “Self-Restriction” set as “Outside Planned Protection” which aims to ensure even low priority protection groups get their share of supply.

 Step 1: Create Supply Protection Extension Rule

  • Go to Fiori App “Configure Supply Protection Extension Rules”
  • Click on “Create” and enter below configuration required in the rule

  • Lets us understand the specifics of each field
    • General Information:
      • Rule Name – Provide a name for the extension rule to be used in “Manage Supply Protection” Fiori App.
    • Restriction Horizon:
      • Format – Here you can specify if you can borrow units from forward and backward time buckets in “Days” or “Time buckets”
      • Backward Period – How many backward time buckets/days you can borrow/consume units from the matching time bucket
      • Forward Period – How many forward time buckets/days you can borrow/consume units from the matching time bucket
    • Consumption:
      • Consumption Reference Type: Here you can specify if you want to consume based on “standard” (i.e. all units consumed only from matching time bucket) or “Restriction Horizon” (apply backward/forward horizons for consumption)
      • Consumption Direction: currently 2 types of consumption directions are support –
        • Backward then Forward – System consumes in sequence: Matching time bucket -> Matching time bucket – 1 -> Matching time bucket – 2 (upto X number of backward time buckets) -> Matching time bucket + 1 -> Matching time bucket + 2 (upto X number of forward time buckets). Any left-over unconsumed quantities after above sequence will be consumed in matching time bucket. For. E.g in above example, if ECOM Sales Order comes in March for 160 units, then system consumes in sequence March (50) -> Feb (50) -> April (50) -> March (10). Final consumptions will be Feb (50), March (60), April (50).
        • Forward then Backward – System consumes in sequence: Matching time bucket -> Matching time bucket + 1 -> Matching time bucket + 2 (upto X number of forward time buckets) -> Matching time bucket - 1 -> Matching time bucket - 2 (upto X number of backward time buckets). Any leftover unconsumed quantities after above sequence will be consumed in matching time bucket. For. E.g in above example, if ECOM Sales Order comes in March for 160 units, then system consumes in sequence March (50) -> April (50) -> Feb (50) -> March (10). Final consumptions will be Feb (50), March (60), April (50).
      • Priority Restriction:
        • Special Handling of Equal Priority – This indicator specifies if restrictions of “equal priority” should be considered only in the “out plan” quantity or both “in plan and out plan” quantities. i.e. If an ECOM order comes for 200 units, then “in plan” quantity is “150” and out plan is “50”:
          • If flag is not set – restriction of “Retail” protection for 300 units will be applicable for both “in plan” and “out plan” quantities
          • If flag is set – restriction of “Retail” protection for 300 units will be applicable only for “out plan” quantities

Step 2: Attach Supply Protection Extension Rule to Supply Protection Object

  • Go to Fiori App “Manage Supply Protection” and define below parameters as shown below: (Note – Attach the extension rule)
  • Note - Set Self Restriction Type as "Restriction Outside Planned Protection"

 

 

  • Activate the supply protection object

Case 1: Let’s take an example where ECOM Sales Order comes in for 200 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “150” (including Feb and April). So, the 200 ECOM order request is checked in 2 steps as shown below:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

150

No restrictions applied

150

Out Plan Quantity

50

-          Equal Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          Low Priority – 400 units from Key Wholesale

-          Self-Restriction – 50 from ECOM April

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

175 units

Case 2: Let’s take an example where RETAIL STO comes in for 200 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “225” (including Feb and April). So, the 200 RETAIL order request is checked in single step:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

200

No restrictions applied

200

Final Confirmations

 

 

200 units

Case 3: Let’s take an example where Key Wholesale Sales Order comes in for 500 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “300” (including Feb and April). So, the 500 Key Wholesale order request checked in two steps as shown below:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

300

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

300

Out Plan Quantity

200

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          Self-Restriction – 100 from Key Wholesale April

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

325 units

Case 4: Let’s take an example where regular Wholesale Sales Order comes in for 500 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since there is no protection for regular wholesale order, all 500 units will be checked in single step:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

Request Quantity

500

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          High Priority – 400 units from Key Wholesale

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

25 units

Hope this blog helps to provide a clear understanding of “Supply Protection Extension Rules” to define appropriate settings to prioritize different channels as per business requirements.
